### Post by aleb on 2007-12-17
If you use TwinView, the NVidia driver can act as a Xinerama server. So if you have TwinView and want your maximized windows to span only one screen, you have to run:
```
sudo nvidia-xconfig --twinview-xinerama-info
```

This command added the last Option element and the SubSection to the following Section:
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option         "metamodes" "DFP-0: 2560x1600 +0+0, DFP-1: 1920x1200 +2560+0"
[B]    Option         "NoTwinViewXineramaInfo" "False"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "nvidia-auto-select"
    EndSubSection[/B]
EndSection
```

Xinerama is still disabled in ServerFlags!

```
Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection
```

I'd like to understand why the Xinerama interface of the NVidia driver is not enabled by default.. 

HTH!


> **Jabb3r said:**
> I'm having exactly the same spanning issues which I can't work out howto solve yet and even when xinerama wasn't enabled it still boots up things like quake wars accross the 2 screens and not even in the propper way which makes things pretty much unplayable.
I read something about programs need to be compiled with the xinerama use flag and then they should span to whichever monitor there on, but I think there must be more to it so will keep digging. Will post back if I find the solution.

Btw on most of theese configs I see this

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

For xinerama to be enabled it should be

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "true"
EndSection

Hope that helps someone.

### Post by picoday on 2007-12-23
Thanks Ziox!

This was the most comprehensive overview of dual heading methods I have found. I'm a newbee to Ubuntu (been a Mac user all my life pretty much). 

Although the methods here (Xinerama in particular) didn't seem to work for me, your howto was really helpful in getting me started (e.g. understanding how important it is to know what graphics HW you have and that solutions are very specific to that).

A method not discussed in your howto (presumably because it was written so long ago) but which works beatifully for me is using xrandr.

I'll post some details for others who might be trying to get that nice external monitor up and running with their laptops.

My configuration:
[LIST]
[*]Lenovo Thinkpad T61 laptop
[*]Intel GMA X3100 integrated graphics
[*]Dell 2407 fps (1920x1200 resolution flatpanel LCD monitor)
[*]Ubuntu Gutsy (7.10)
[/LIST]

If you have an intel integrated graphics card than this will probably work for you too.

Step 0: startup with no extra monitors attached.

Step 1: You need to make sure to have the right graphics drivers set up.

In System >> Administration >> Screens and Graphics 
On the Tab "Graphics Card"

You should see two graphics cards make sure *both* have selected the "Intel Experimentla modesetting" drivers. (This may not actually be necessry, but that's what I did and it worked).

Step 2: Check your laptop screen

```
$ xrandr -q
Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 1280 x 800, maximum 1920 x 2000
VGA disconnected (normal left inverted right)
LVDS connected 1280x800+0+0 (normal left inverted right) 331mm x 207mm
   1280x800       60.0*+   60.0     50.0  
   1280x768       60.0  
   1024x768       60.0  
   800x600        60.3  
   640x480        59.9  
TMDS-1 disconnected (normal left inverted right)

```

The above means that only the LVDS (laptop screen is connected now).

It also shows you information about what xrandr thinks are the resolutions and sync frequencies it thinks this screen supports.

Step 3: Check extra screen

Plugin in your extra monitor and now run xrandr again

```
$ xrandr -q
Screen 0: minimum 320 x 200, current 1280 x 800, maximum 1920 x 2000
VGA connected (normal left inverted right)
   1920x1200      60.0 +
   1920x1200@60   60.0  
   ... some stuff deleted for space saving ...
   720x400        70.1  
LVDS connected 1280x800+0+0 (normal left inverted right) 331mm x 207mm
   1280x800       60.0*+   60.0     50.0  
   1280x768       60.0  
   1024x768       60.0  
   800x600        60.3  
   640x480        59.9  
TMDS-1 disconnected (normal left inverted right)

```

If all went well xrandr should have detected your second screen now (and may even have decide to activate it).

If it hasn't activated it you can try this:

```
$ xrandr --output VGA --auto 
```

Note: you might need to replace VGA by whatever xrandr detected your second display (maybe it is TMDS-1 for example

Step 4: Dual Head

```
$ xrandr --output LVDS --below VGA
```

Note 1: Instead of --below. you can also use --right-of --left-of or --above

The first time I tried this it didn't work for me, I got an error: "xrandr: screen cannot be larger than ...". To fix this you need to do one more thing by editing your xorg.conf file.

Use your favorite editor to do this. For example I like emacs so:

```
$ sudo emacs /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```

Now find the Screen section and add or modify a "Virtual" line. This specifies the maximum dimension of your combined "dual" screen. 

```
Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Intel Corporation Mobile GM965/GL960 Integrated Graphics Controller"
	Monitor		"DELL 2407WFP"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	24
		**Virtual	1920	2000**
		Modes		"1920x1200@60"	"1680x1050@75"	 ...
	EndSubSection
EndSection

```

The "virtual" line should give screen dimensions that are big enough for the "sum" of both your screens. For example, I want my 1280x800 LVDS below my 1920x1200 VGA display. To be able to do this I had increase the size frp, 1920x1200 to 1920x**2000** (2000 = 1200 + 800). If you want the screens side by side then you have to increase the horizontal dimensions instead.

After editing xorg.conf. Logout and login again to restart X for this change to take effect.

Now the command  

```
$ xrandr --output LVDS --below VGA
```

should work.

Some other useful xrandr commands:

```
xrandr --output LVDS --off
``` Turns the laptop screen off.
```
xrandr --output LVDS --auto
``` Turn laptop screen on (using default resolution)
```
xrandr --output LVDS --same-as VGA
``` Mirror displays (to work well you also need to set the screen resolutions to be the same.

Others useful info
[LIST]
[*] [http://lilserenity.wordpress.com/2007/10/15/ecstatic-xrandr-12-means-decent-linux-screen-management-at-last/](http://lilserenity.wordpress.com/2007/10/15/ecstatic-xrandr-12-means-decent-linux-screen-management-at-last/)
[*] man xrandr
[/LIST]

### Post by sean4u on 2007-12-31
Not sure where else to post this. I've got an ATI X1300 in a Dell laptop Inspiron 6400. I was happily using an external monitor to its right and bigdesktop to give me one wide desktop.
We need a bit more table space, so I moved the external LCD above and behind the laptop panel and used aticonfig --dtop vertical,reverse to redo my xorg.conf. For frustrating hours, I tried different edits to xorg.conf, but only got either bad resolutions on the external panel, or more often, a cloned desktop.

Not sure where I read the comment, but I saw someone write that they thought it was okay when gdm was showing the login dialog, but not otherwise. That was the same for me. I use xfce to manage my desktop, but when I switched to a Gnome session, the desktop continued to span both monitors, as I wanted. I couldn't find a configuration dialog that fixed the xfce problem, so eventually deleted the xfce configuration folders in my $HOME/.config folder. Next login, xfce worked fine with the vertically stacked monitors.

Since I haven't seen this info elsewhere, I post it in the hope it helps someone.

---

    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 70.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 160.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "PHILIPS 107S"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 70.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 160.0
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "nVidia Corporation G72 [GeForce 7300 LE]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    Option         "AddARGBVisuals" "True"
    Option         "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "True"
    Option         "NoLogo" "True"
    Option         "TwinView" "True"
    Option         "TwinViewOrientation" "RightOf"
    Option         "UseEdidFreqs" "True"
    Option         "NoPowerConnectorCheck"
    Option         "MetaModes" "NULL,1280x1024; 1280x1024,1280x1024; 1280x1024, NULL"
    Option         "UseDisplayDevice" "CRT, CRT" #replace 'string' with either 'DFP' (Digital flat panel connected via DVI port), 'CRT' (any monitor that is connected via VGA ports), or 'TV'
    BusID          "PCI:2:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 LE"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "nVidia Corporation G72 [GeForce 7300 LE]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Screen"

# Removed Option "metamodes" "CRT: 1280x1024_60 +0+0, DFP: 1024x768 +1280+0; CRT: 1024x768 +0+0, DFP: 1024x768 +1024+0"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option         "metamodes" "CRT: 1280x1024_60 +0+0, DFP: nvidia-auto-select +1280+0; CRT: 1024x768 +0+0, DFP: 1024x768 +1024+0"
EndSection


---

### Post by fredBuntu on 2008-01-25
Hi everybody, 

I do not know if this is mentioned here somewhere, I suppose it is, but I did not find it on the first page and I would like to simply add this very important note:

[B]A - check your cables ! and make sure they work properly before attempting whatsoever

[/B]I have spent the last two nights palying with my fresh mythbuntu, trying to get dualview with my tv and nvidia, I have written dozens of possible xorg.conf and all of that to finally find out it was a stupid cable problem.

Incriminated cable is the svhs to reca converter that you can buy cheap at every store or online, do not go for the cheap solution ! It does not work properly.

Changed the cable over lunch and voilà ! all fine ! Except my 16/9 does not like the screen size I set, but that is nothing.

Hope it will help some of you !

---

### Post by JoanneDisFan on 2008-01-27
I'm using an older system running a Ati Rage 3D Mach64 graphics card.  The card has a s video out and a regular monitor output jack.  If possible I would like to hook up to my TV to use it as a monitor on occasion.  To play games, watch movies, etc. I've been looking at all the various options and from what I can find none of the options support this old of a card.  Can anyone confirm or deny this? 

I've tried Xinerama, Ati tv-out, and the flgrx drivers.  So far nothing has worked.  I have been able to plug in the tv via s-video and on boot up I can see the boot up sequence, but as soon as ubuntu starts to load I lose the graphics.  Last time I tried I got so frustrated I just gave up, but someone suggested looking into bigdesktop.  But I don't think that the fglrx driver support the mach64 card.  Anyone got any good suggestions other than buy a new card or better yet a new computer.

Thanks, 
Joanne

### Post by Horza on 2008-01-28
A very basic question about the starting post on this thread:  at what point does one plug in the second monitor?  What I did, in an unsuccesful attempt to use the merged frame buffer method, is:

  a) unplug to go thru the reconfiguration (but did that in X, which may have been a mistake)

  b) edit xorg.conf & save the changed versione to my desktop

  c) reboot into recovery mode and copy the new xorg.conf to /etc/X11

  d) shut down

  e) plug in second monitor

  f) start up again

And a slightly different one; when running dual monitors, should there be one screen and one monitor section, or two of each?

What am I missing? Can someone lend me a hand? Would be really appreciated? I'll probaby get a 17" monitor then see if I can get another video card and see if I can't have 3 monitors on the go. Why? Because I need a hobby.

Thanks in advance.

### Post by Horza on 2008-01-29
> **Ziox said:**
> [B][SIZE=4]Features of this HowTo:

[/SIZE][/B] 

...
        [LEFT]*[COLOR=Red]**[FONT=Verdana]So, unplug all your monitors except for one, then execute the following command:[/FONT]**[/COLOR]*[/LEFT]
        [LEFT][FONT=Verdana]```
**sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg**
```[/FONT][/COLOR][/LEFT]


Is the unplugging step really necessary?  The reconfiguration program doesn't seem to have much in the way of autodetection capacity (maybe none?)

> 

        [LEFT][FONT=Verdana]Hopefully these guides have been helpful.

[/FONT][/LEFT]


Yes, very.

>    
[LIST]
[*][FONT=Verdana]   [COLOR=YellowGreen] **Do not do your config inside of X11. Before you login, press Ctrl-Alt-F1 and login to the text console. There, shutdown the graphical login like this: sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop. This way you can edit the xorg.conf and test your setup one piece at a time. The command startx launches X11 as though you had logged in and drops you back to the text console when you 'logout' of the GUI.**[/COLOR][/FONT][/LIST]


I find the methods for starting and stopping X to  rather cumbersome and unreliable; editing in X, saving xorg.conf, and just rebooting seems to be easy and robust.
]

### Post by Nepo47 on 2008-01-30
Thanks so much for this help!!! I used Xinerama to set up two monitors and two different video cards. Here's a copy of my xorg.conf for anyone that might need help as well...

# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"		"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"0 Intel Corporation 82865G Integrated Graphics Controller"
	Driver		"i810"
	BusID		"PCI:0:2:0"
	Option		"UseFBDev"		"true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"1 nVidia Corporation NV5M64 [RIVA TNT2 Model 64/Model 64 Pro]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	BusID		"PCI:1:2:0"
	Option		"UseFBDev"		"true"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"0 EMPREX LM170"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	30-80
	VertRefresh	56-75
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"1 EMPREX LM170"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	30-80
	VertRefresh	56-75
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"0 Intel Corporation 82865G Integrated Graphics Controller"
	Monitor		"0 EMPREX LM170"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768"
	EndSubSection
EndSection


Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Second Screen"
	Device		"1 nVidia Corporation NV5M64 [RIVA TNT2 Model 64/Model 64 Pro]"
	Monitor		"1 EMPREX LM170"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen	     0	"Default Screen"
	Screen	     1	"Second Screen" Rightof "Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	Option "Xinerama" "true"

# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

### Post by Sam Lars on 2008-03-15
I had that same problem, but I finally figured it out.
I was starting X on one screen and using nvidia-settings to make it two.
Now I've started X with two screens, and the line
```
    Option	"NoTwinViewXineramaInfo"	"False"
```
in the Screen section of my xorg.conf.  Windows now know where to maximize and appear with respect to the two screens.

---

### Post by _Stevie_ on 2008-03-15
hi Sam Lars!

im glad theres a solution. did you use "twinview" or "seperate x screens"?

---

### Post by Sam Lars on 2008-03-15
TwinView.  I used nvidia-settings to save a new xorg.conf (not merge), and then added that line.

---

### Post by _Stevie_ on 2008-03-15
ill try that, thanks!

edit: that didnt work :(
i still have the gnome bar spanned on both screens.

edit:
okay i solved it!

it is important to take a look the settings that nvidia-settings shows when clicking on "preview"
as seen on the screenhot.
i did mistake during all the time... i configured 2 different screens.
whereas the nvidia "wizard" configures only 1 screen and extends it to a second.
for my tv-out i used the following settings:

```
Section "Monitor"
    # HorizSync source: xconfig, VertRefresh source: xconfig
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "TV-0"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 72.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce Go 7600"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option		"NoLogo"		"True"
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option  	   "TVOutFormat" "COMPOSITE"
    Option         "metamodes" "TV: 1024x768 +1440+0, DFP: 1440x900 +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

hope this may help some other users.

### Post by mistermic on 2008-05-15
I found a simple solution for a new Dell Precision T5400, with Nvidia FX 570 graphics card...after accepting the proprietary Nvidia driver on install, and installing every compiz* package that looked cool, with Synaptic package manager, I then **installed nvidia-settings (sudo apt-get install nvidia-settings)**, and after install, I ran that from the **command line (sudo nvidia-settings)**.  It pops up a small window...that you can resize to see a bunch of options.  Highlight "X Server Display Configuration" and play with it...it showed my second screen, that was disabled by default.  After enabling, it rocked!  I tried Xinerama, but it wouldn't allow the Desktop Cube effect...  so I have 2 separate X displays (one per screen) but the mouse glides to each screen, and I even have synergy running to a laptop, so it's like 3 screens...

---

### Post by twistdhood on 2008-05-17
New here to the Ubuntu world, just installed. I'm from a RHEL history of installs so everything is kinda new to me. But I'm pretty capable.

I have a distinctive issue and hoping someone can help. I've followed the instructions on setting up dual monitors and have a strange scenario. I cannot get the "cloning" feature to turn off. HOWEVER, heres the interesting part. At the login screen it appears correct, my mouse moves from one screen to the other and no movement of either window is seen, so it appears correct. When I log in however, the desktop is duped/cloned and everything exists on both screens. Now I've tried the option clone false flag and it only gives a bad configuration on [ctrl]+[alt]+[backspace] and I'm forced to copy my backup back to working order. On top of that, the GUI feature for screen resolution confirms/agrees that my clone feature is off.

I've been browsing forums, google, etc for 2 days now so I'm looking for direct help here from anyone who's willing...

I have attached my xorg.conf.

Thanks!
~TwistD


EDIT: 
Ok, didn't realize the aitconfig was being put in my cwd, so I copied that in and whoosh, it all works. SO, n real problem here, we can all go about our business as usual.

RESOLUTION: (with no advanced graphics) 
$ sudo aticonfig --initial
$ sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=right
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--
$ sudo aticonfig --dtop=horizontal
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--

Looking for the post on how to get the accelerated graphics on one of the screens.

### Post by Denkogami on 2008-05-28
After a lot of effort I finally got a driver installed for my 9600 GT and, as I thought, most of my other display related problems fit into place (woohoo!).

AKA, I now have dual screens rigged without one being the clone of the other. However, there is one minor hiccup. That is that my main monitor is new and runs at 1680 x 1050, and the second monitor is my old one, running at 1024 x 768. Obviously, the same wallpaper doesn't work for both :( So I was wondering if there was a way to get separate backgrounds.

I'm using the "Separate X Screen" function in NVidia as opposed to TwinView (which just made things odd). 

In case necessary, or useful, here is my xorg.conf:
```

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
    Screen      1  "Screen1" RightOf "Screen0"
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "v4l"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "ca"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Configured Monitor"
    VendorName     "Samsung"
    ModelName      "Samsung SyncMaster 226BW (Digital)"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 81.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 75.0
    Gamma           1

### Post by Fergus_McSnort on 2008-06-02
Well here's my experience thus far, and a request for suggestions.

Video card:  ASUS EAH 3450, uses ATI Radeon HD 3450 GPU, 256 MB.
Monitors:  Identical Orion (seemingly generic, run of the mill) CRTs.
Motherboard:  Shuttle SG31G2

Had installed Feisty Fawn on my original system which had different ATI-based video card and older Shuttle motherboard.  Feisty recognized the card and installed an appropriate driver, a simple edit to xorg.conf had the dual display working just fine.  

Had a motherboard failure (had upgraded to Hardy Heron by this time), and I replaced both the motherboard and the video card. Rebooted, and Ubuntu recognized most new things, but not the video card.  

After much trial and tribulation, sorrow and disappointment, shedding of tears and wringing of hands, and following every possible trail on this and related threads, I have come to an imperfect but usable end.

Dual display is working, BUT:

1.  Windows dragged to the 2nd monitor become slow and sluggish.
2.  Screen saver pops up on the main monitor, but not the 2nd, and essentially locks the main monitor. Cannot release screen saver once it appears without restarting X Windows. So, screen saver cannot be used.
3.  Monitor power management does not work reliably. Once in a great while it powers down the monitors after 30 minutes (current setting), but not usually.

Below is a copy of the current xorg.conf.  Thanks for any assistance or suggestions.

#********************************************
Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc104"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"0 Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Busid		"PCI:1:0:0"
	Screen	0
	Option		"DDCMode"	"True"
	Option		"MonitorLayout"	"CRT,CRT"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"1 Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Busid		"PCI:1:0:0"
	Screen	1
	Option		"DDCMode"	"True"
	Option		"MonitorLayout"	"CRT,CRT"
EndSection


Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Main Screen"
	Device		"0 Configured Video Device"
	Monitor		"Main Monitor"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	1
		Modes		"1024x768"
	EndSubSection
	# Subsections for other display depths not shown...
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Second Screen"
	Device		"1 Configured Video Device"
	Monitor		"Second Monitor"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	1
		Modes		"1024x768"
	EndSubSection
	# Subsections for other display depths not shown...
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen 0 "Main Screen"
  screen 1 "Second Screen" rightof "Main Screen"
	Option		"Xinerama"	"true"
EndSection
Section "Module"
	Load		"glx"
EndSection

### Post by Fergus_McSnort on 2008-06-02
> **Fergus_McSnort said:**
> Well here's my experience thus far, and a request for suggestions.
EndSection

One more quirk I forgot to mention.  You'll notice there's no "Monitor" section in the above xorg.conf.  If I include any bit of the following, Ubuntu produces a "graphics device not detected" error on loading X windows:

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier "Main Monitor"
	Options "DPMS"
End Section

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier "Second Monitor"
	Options "DPMS"
End Section

### Post by abhiroopb on 2008-06-14
UPDATE on Hardy (with nvidia):

I have just discovered that Xinemera solves my problems better than TwinView without (till now) any problems.

Basically the problems I had with TwinView were:
1. Full screen games would go over both screens.
2. If I ever disconnected my monitor from my laptop it would look as though I had half a screen on my laptop (and my one panel that is on my big monitor would be stretched across my laptop screen), so I would have to reset my screen settings.

With Xinemera in hardy my screens are treated as two separate screens so:
1. full screen games only play on the active monitor
2. If I disconnect my monitor and restart X all panels and desktop icons appear solely on my laptop screen as though there was never a monitor.

In gutsy Xinemera would give problems, but in Hardy it seems like a superior option to TwinView.

---

### Post by LxAxKxI on 2008-06-16
> **twistdhood said:**
> New here to the Ubuntu world, just installed. I'm from a RHEL history of installs so everything is kinda new to me. But I'm pretty capable.

I have a distinctive issue and hoping someone can help. I've followed the instructions on setting up dual monitors and have a strange scenario. I cannot get the "cloning" feature to turn off. HOWEVER, heres the interesting part. At the login screen it appears correct, my mouse moves from one screen to the other and no movement of either window is seen, so it appears correct. When I log in however, the desktop is duped/cloned and everything exists on both screens. Now I've tried the option clone false flag and it only gives a bad configuration on [ctrl]+[alt]+[backspace] and I'm forced to copy my backup back to working order. On top of that, the GUI feature for screen resolution confirms/agrees that my clone feature is off.

I've been browsing forums, google, etc for 2 days now so I'm looking for direct help here from anyone who's willing...

I have attached my xorg.conf.

Thanks!
~TwistD


EDIT: 
Ok, didn't realize the aitconfig was being put in my cwd, so I copied that in and whoosh, it all works. SO, n real problem here, we can all go about our business as usual.

RESOLUTION: (with no advanced graphics) 
$ sudo aticonfig --initial
$ sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=right
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--
$ sudo aticonfig --dtop=horizontal
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--

Looking for the post on how to get the accelerated graphics on one of the screens.

Thanks twistdhood,
Now finally I have dual screen ;-).

I follow you advice but only up to this step :
> **twistdhood said:**
> 
$ sudo aticonfig --initial
$ sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=right
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--

up to this , when i loog in , I have two screen and no clone, but when i open something (console) on main screen i cannot move to the second screen is go to the end of the  first screen and like is bang on the wall, cannot go to second screen.
(sorry for my english).
so if i follow your next step :
> **twistdhood said:**
> 
$ sudo aticonfig --dtop=horizontal
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--

Then when I log in i have dual screen but is clone.
so what i did, i open my xorg file after this steps :
$ sudo aticonfig --initial
$ sudo aticonfig --initial=dual-head --screen-layout=right
$ [ctrl] + [alt] + [backspace]
--log back in--
and just add this line in 

Option "Xinerama" "true" in 

Section "ServerLayout"
Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	Screen      1   "aticonfig-Screen[1]" RightOf "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
EndSection

and a save file log out, log in and i have my dual 'perfect' screen.

here is my xorg file :

> 
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)

### Post by Fergus_McSnort on 2008-06-20
> **Fergus_McSnort said:**
> Well here's my experience thus far, and a request for suggestions.


Just for the record, I revisted this problem after I noticed that the fglxr driver had been automatically updated, along with a few other graphics-related files.  

I had no luck with mergeFB, but was able to get BigDesktop working pretty well, which fixed the above problem.

### Post by JoneYee on 2008-07-24
cat /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"vmmouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
EndSection
```

I have backed-up my xorg.conf to xorg.laptoponly.prod for now  

My big question is this.  In the Device area, I was expecting to see something along the lines of:

```
Section "Device"
    Identifier    "NAME YOU'RE GIVING THE CARD"
    Driver        "DRIVER"
    BusID        "BUS LOCATION"
    Option        "OPTION" "OPTION VALUE"
    Screen        NUMBER OF THE MONITOR, STARTING AT 0
EndSection

```

However I am not seeing that, and I am apprehensive about makign changes here that I dont know.

Why is  my Device section so empty with respect to my displays.

I am attempting to use the external port on my HP DV1331SE for a dual monitor configuration, and stumbled right out of the gate.
